title: SEGMENT 15 — Clinical Illumina: diagnostics, prenatal screening, tumor panels

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — Clinical Illumina: diagnostics, prenatal screening, tumor panels
We've talked about the technology, the chemistry, the cost.
Let me spend a segment on what Illumina sequencing actually does in clinical medicine today, because the answer is: a great deal.
Illumina sequencing isn't just a research tool.
It's a routine clinical assay, used millions of times per year for specific diagnostic purposes.
Let me walk you through the main categories.
Rare disease diagnosis through exome and genome sequencing.
A child is born with a constellation of unusual features — developmental delay, unusual facial features, organ malformations — that don't match any known disease.

The clinical workup before NGS would have involved targeted gene testing, often dozens of single-gene tests done sequentially, costing tens of thousands of dollars and taking months to years, with a diagnosis rate of maybe 10 to 20 percent.
Today, that child gets whole-exome or whole-genome sequencing as a first-line test.

The lab sequences the proband — the patient — plus both parents (a so-called "trio"), looks for variants in the proband that are absent from the parents or that match an inheritance pattern, and filters those candidates against databases of known disease-associated variants.
The diagnosis rate jumps to 30 to 50 percent in some categories, and the time to diagnosis drops from years to weeks.
For some critically ill newborns in NICUs, rapid whole-genome sequencing turnaround in 24 to 48 hours has become standard at some major children's hospitals.
Non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT).
This is a startling clinical application.
Pregnant women have small amounts of fetal DNA circulating in their bloodstream — cell-free fetal DNA shed from the placenta.

A blood draw from the mother can be sequenced, and by looking at the fraction of reads mapping to each chromosome, you can detect fetal aneuploidies — Down syndrome (trisomy 21), Patau syndrome (trisomy 13), Edwards syndrome (trisomy 18) — without any invasive procedure.

The technique relies on the statistical power of sequencing millions of fragments and noticing that, in a Down syndrome pregnancy, the chromosome 21 reads are slightly elevated in proportion, reflecting the extra copy in the fetal portion.

NIPT has largely replaced the older serum-marker screens, has detection rates above 99 percent for the common trisomies, and now accounts for a substantial fraction of routine prenatal care in many countries.
It's one of the largest-volume clinical Illumina applications, with millions of tests run per year worldwide.
Cancer tumor profiling.
Modern oncology routinely uses Illumina sequencing to profile tumor genetics for treatment selection.
The clinical question is: what mutations does this patient's tumor have that might be drug-targetable?
A biopsy is taken, DNA and sometimes RNA are extracted, libraries are prepared, and a focused panel of cancer-relevant genes — typically a few hundred — is sequenced at high depth.

The result identifies actionable mutations: a BRAF V600E in a melanoma might predict response to BRAF inhibitors; an EGFR mutation in a lung cancer predicts response to EGFR inhibitors; HER2 amplification suggests trastuzumab.
The use of these panels has become standard of care for many cancer types in the major treatment centers.
There's also a more sensitive use case called liquid biopsy — sequencing the small amount of tumor DNA that circulates in the blood of cancer patients (analogous to cell-free fetal DNA in pregnancy).

Liquid biopsies can monitor disease burden over time without repeat tissue biopsies, detect emerging resistance mutations during treatment, and increasingly serve as a screening tool to detect cancers early before they're symptomatic.
The technical challenge is that circulating tumor DNA is rare — often less than 0.1 percent of total circulating DNA — so extremely deep sequencing and careful error-suppression methods are required.
Companies like Guardant, Natera, and Grail (formed inside Illumina, briefly acquired, then divested) have built businesses around this application.
Carrier screening and pharmacogenomics.

Many couples planning a pregnancy now undergo carrier screening for recessive genetic diseases — sequencing a panel of disease-associated genes in both parents to assess the risk that any future child would inherit two copies of a disease allele.

Pharmacogenomic testing, which sequences genes affecting drug metabolism, is increasingly used to guide drug selection and dosing — particularly for psychiatric medications, anticoagulants, and certain chemotherapies.
These are all panel-based Illumina applications running in clinical labs at scale.
Infectious disease and microbial sequencing.

Hospitals use Illumina sequencing for hospital-acquired infection tracking — sequencing the genomes of isolates from different patients to determine whether an outbreak is one strain spreading or many independent infections.

Public health labs use it for foodborne illness investigation — the PulseNet system in the US tracks pathogens like Salmonella and Listeria using whole-genome sequencing of clinical and food isolates.

And of course COVID-19 surveillance famously used Illumina sequencing at unprecedented scale, with millions of viral genomes contributing to real-time variant tracking through GISAID and similar databases.
The pattern across these clinical applications: Illumina sequencing has gone from a research tool used by a few academics to a clinical workhorse used in mainstream medicine.
The decisions made on the basis of Illumina data — drug selection in cancer, prenatal management, diagnosis of pediatric rare disease — affect millions of patients per year.
The chemistry that started in a Cambridge pub now drives clinical decisions in every major hospital.
That progression — from invention to widespread routine clinical use — took about twenty-five years, which is reasonably fast for a transformative biomedical technology.
For all this clinical use, regulatory approval matters.

Illumina has obtained FDA approval for specific instruments and reagents used as diagnostic devices — the NextSeq 550Dx, for example, is the IVD (in vitro diagnostic) version of the NextSeq, with regulatory clearance for specific clinical assays.
The chemistry is the same; the regulatory documentation, the quality systems, the labeling are different.
As clinical adoption deepens, the regulatory side of the platform has become an increasingly significant business consideration.
